summary.brief: Falletta v. Norman. Medical malpractice case involving cardiology.

evidence_index.text: In February 2016, James A. Falletta (decedent) arrived at the emergency department at Millard Fillmore Suburban Hospital (Millard Fillmore) complaining of burning in his chest and shortness of breath.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Decedent died of a myocardial rupture due to myocardial infarction the day after he arrived at Millard Fillmore.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Decedent's medical records included a note that an interventional cardiologist was contacted, but the name of the physician was not listed.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The order denied the motion of defendant David M. Zlotnick, M.D. to dismiss the amended complaint against him.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We nevertheless reject Zlotnick's contention that the court erred in denying his motion inasmuch as we conclude that plaintiff raised a triable issue of fact with respect to the applicability of the relation back doctrine.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: Zlotnick does not dispute that the first prong is met here, and we reject his contention that plaintiff failed to raise an issue of fact with respect to the second and third prongs.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: Plaintiff raised a triable issue of fact whether Kaleida and Zlotnick are united in interest, and thus there is a triable issue of fact whether the second prong of the relation back doctrine is satisfied.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

evidence_index.text: With respect to the third prong, plaintiff established that her failure to include Zlotnick was a mistake and not the result of a strategy to obtain a tactical advantage.
evidence_index.speaker: Judge's analysis

initial_state.chief_complaint: Burning in chest and shortness of breath
initial_state.history_of_present_illness: Patient presented to the emergency department with burning chest pain and shortness of breath.

focused_exam.cardiovascular: Examination mentioned but findings not specified

reveal.result_summary: Consultation was requested but not documented in detail
reveal.clinical_significance: Critical for determining need for intervention
reveal.decision_impact: Could have led to timely intervention

timeline_phases.name: Emergency Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents to ED with acute symptoms

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient in ED with suspected myocardial infarction
decision_points.prompt: What should be the next step in management?

options.clinical_reasoning: Standard care for suspected myocardial infarction with potential complications

actual_action_defendant.description: Defendant continued with medical management only

expected_action_court.description: Immediate cardiac catheterization should have been ordered
expected_action_court.standard_of_care_basis: Expert evidence established need for intervention

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Failure to escalate care based on symptoms
explanation.what_should_have_happened: Immediate intervention could have prevented myocardial rupture

patient_outcome.description: Patient died of myocardial rupture due to myocardial infarction

key_findings: Breach of duty in failing to escalate care
Causation established - earlier intervention could have prevented death

malpractice_determination.point_of_failure: Failure to escalate care with cardiac intervention
malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Timely intervention could have prevented myocardial rupture

allegations.allegation: Failure to escalate care with cardiac intervention
